interactions.summary: Jiang explains why the Keju is unfair and discretionary: it confines elite ambition to decades of study, then lets the emperor decide who actually receives office.

He adds the bureaucracy's monopoly over literacy and culture: unlike Egypt's simplification toward alphabetic literacy, China makes literary Chinese harder, and Confucian hierarchy suppresses mobile or rival status paths.

speaker_notes.note: All focus refs are Jiang monologue.

claims.claim: The Keju is restricted to elite families because mastery of literary Chinese requires money, tutoring, leisure, and decades of preparation.
The political function of the Keju is to concentrate elite ambition away from rebellion, invention, and independent literature.
Exam success is not the same as political success because the emperor retains discretionary authority over appointment and promotion.
China is unique in Jiang's telling because it made literacy harder over time, turning literary Chinese into a bureaucratic rent-seeking language.
Confucianism functions as bureaucratic culture: it legitimizes status hierarchy, ancestor-bound locality, harmony, and the suppression of trade and innovation.

signature_moments.moment: The exam is an ambition trap: it consumes elite energy so it does not become rebellion, invention, or rival literature.
signature_moments.source_phrase: concentrate the ambition and energy of the elite families into the Keju
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the Keju a psychological and political function beyond selection.

signature_moments.moment: Literary Chinese becomes contract language: a difficulty machine that forces others to pay rent to the class that can read it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a language that only bureaucrats can understand
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns literacy from a developmental tool into a monopoly technology.

signature_moments.moment: Merchants and artists are demoted because trade and art make rival futures possible.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the artists... can promote new ideas... merchants... can accumulate wealth
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves Jiang's claim that status hierarchy is also an anti-innovation design.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Classical/literary Chinese, treated by Jiang as a hard elite skill and bureaucratic monopoly language.
Jiang's pejorative label for literary Chinese as a language only bureaucrats can understand.
Creating a specialized system only one's group can use so others must pay for access.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang sketches a long language-history contrast from Egyptian pictograms to alphabetic simplification, then contrasts China as increasing complexity.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR renders Jiang's 'bureaucratese' as variants like 'bureaucratat' and 'bureaucraties'; semantic usage is clear from the contract-language analogy.
